mainEntity.name: What is the ideal C:N ratio for composting?

acceptedAnswer.text: The ideal carbon-to-nitrogen ratio for composting is 25:1 to 30:1. At this ratio, microorganisms can efficiently break down materials. Too much carbon (over 40:1) slows decomposition; too much nitrogen (under 20:1) creates odor.

mainEntity.name: What are greens and browns in composting?

acceptedAnswer.text: Greens are nitrogen-rich materials: grass clippings (20:1), food scraps (15:1), coffee grounds (20:1), and fresh manure. Browns are carbon-rich: dry leaves (60:1), cardboard (350:1), straw (80:1), and wood chips (400:1).

mainEntity.name: How much brown material do I need per green?

acceptedAnswer.text: A general rule is 3 parts browns to 1 part greens by volume. However, the exact ratio depends on specific materials. High-carbon browns like cardboard need less volume than dry leaves to balance the same amount of greens.

mainEntity.name: Why does my compost smell bad?

acceptedAnswer.text: Bad smell usually means too much nitrogen (greens) and not enough carbon (browns). The C:N ratio is likely below 20:1. Add brown materials like leaves, straw, or shredded cardboard and turn the pile to add oxygen.

mainEntity.name: How big should a compost pile be?

acceptedAnswer.text: A minimum size of 3×3×3 feet (1 cubic yard) is needed to retain heat for active decomposition. The ideal size is 4×4×4 feet. Larger piles can go anaerobic in the center if not turned regularly.

Hot composting with proper C:N ratio, moisture, and turning takes 1-3 months. Cold composting (passive piling) takes 6-12 months. Vermicomposting (worms) takes 3-6 months. The ideal C:N ratio speeds up all methods.

howItWorks:
How the Composting Ratio Calculator Works

This calculator computes the weighted carbon-to-nitrogen ratio of your compost mix based on material types and amounts.

C:N = (Green C × Green Amt + Brown C × Brown Amt) ÷ Total N
  • Ideal range: 25:1 to 30:1 carbon-to-nitrogen
  • Too high (>40:1): Add greens — pile decomposes slowly
  • Too low (<20:1): Add browns — pile will smell and get slimy
  • Volume: Green + brown amounts in gallons, converted to cubic feet
  • Minimum pile: 3×3×3 ft (27 cu ft) for heat retention
faqHTML:
Frequently Asked Questions

What is the ideal C:N ratio for composting?

The ideal carbon-to-nitrogen ratio is 25:1 to 30:1. At this balance, microbes can efficiently decompose materials. Ratios above 40:1 slow decomposition; below 20:1 cause bad odors from excess nitrogen.

What are greens and browns in composting?

Greens are nitrogen-rich materials like grass clippings, food scraps, and coffee grounds. Browns are carbon-rich materials like dry leaves, cardboard, straw, and wood chips. You need both for effective composting.

How much brown material do I need per green?

Roughly 3 parts browns to 1 part greens by volume is a good starting point. The exact amount depends on the specific materials — high-carbon browns like cardboard need less volume than leaves.

Why does my compost smell bad?

A smelly pile usually has too much nitrogen (C:N below 20:1). Add brown materials like shredded cardboard, dry leaves, or straw. Turn the pile to reintroduce oxygen, which also reduces odor.

How big should a compost pile be?

Minimum 3x3x3 feet (27 cubic feet) to generate and retain heat for active composting. Ideal is 4x4x4 feet. Piles larger than 5x5x5 can go anaerobic in the center without frequent turning.

How long does composting take?

Hot composting with the right C:N ratio, moisture, and regular turning takes 1-3 months. Cold (passive) composting takes 6-12 months. The correct C:N ratio is the biggest factor in speeding things up.

sections.title: What Is the Composting Ratio Calculator?
sections.body: The Composting Ratio Calculator is a free online tool designed for users who need quick, accurate calculations in the practical calculation space. By entering your green material, amount, brown material, you get instant results including current c:n ratio, target range, recommendation. No formulas to memorize, no spreadsheets to build — just enter your numbers and get the answer in seconds. Whether you're a beginner or experienced professional, this calculator saves you time and eliminates guesswork.

sections.title: Why This Calculation Matters
sections.body: Getting current c:n ratio right can make the difference between success and costly mistakes. In practical calculation, small errors compound quickly. Manual calculations are error-prone and time-consuming, especially under pressure. This calculator applies proven formulas used by users worldwide, giving you confidence that your numbers are correct. Use it to get accurate results with precision and avoid common pitfalls that trip up beginners.

sections.title: When Should You Use This Calculator?
sections.body: This tool is most useful when you know your green material and need to find the right current c:n ratio. It's also great for quick estimates before committing to a decision, and to double-check manual calculations or professional quotes, and when comparing different scenarios side by side. Bookmark this page and come back whenever you need a fast, reliable answer — the calculator is always free and requires no signup.

faq.question: Is the Composting Ratio Calculator free to use?
faq.answer: Yes, completely free with no signup required. Use it as many times as you need — there are no limits or hidden fees.

faq.question: How accurate is this calculator?
faq.answer: This calculator uses standard practical calculation formulas trusted by users. Results are reliable estimates for planning purposes. For critical decisions, we recommend consulting a qualified professional to verify.

faq.question: What green material should I enter?
faq.answer: Enter the most accurate green material value you have available. If you're estimating, use a conservative figure. You can always run the calculator again with different values to see how changes affect the results.

faq.question: Can I use this calculator on my phone?
faq.answer: Yes, the Composting Ratio Calculator is fully responsive and works on any device — phone, tablet, or desktop. No app download needed.

faq.question: What does current c:n ratio mean in my results?
faq.answer: The current c:n ratio value represents the primary output of the calculation based on your inputs. Use it as a baseline figure for your practical calculation planning and decision-making.
